title: The Closed Door
subtitle: A Samaritan town refuses to receive the Lord on His way to Jerusalem

stillness: Be still, and gather yourself with your God.
Possess yourself in peace and in tranquility of heart.
Grieve over nothing that befalls you, save sin alone.
gospel: That His coming might not be hidden,
He sent His messengers before His face,
into a city of the Samaritans,
And going in, they entered to prepare for Him.
But they did not receive Him,
because His face was of one going up to Jerusalem.
And His disciples James and John, seeing it,
said: Lord, wilt Thou that we bid fire come down from heaven and consume them?
And turning, He rebuked them, saying:
You know not of what spirit you are.
behold: See the Lord Jesus journeying toward Jerusalem.
His very face is set as one going up to the feast of the Pasch.
By His garments the Samaritans knew Him and His disciples to be Jews,
and the Jews were hateful to them, who would not eat nor drink with them.
They envied the temple of Jerusalem, and grudged those who went there to adore,
saying the place of adoration was among them on Mount Garizim, and not in Jerusalem.
And so, seeing the Lord going on toward His enemies, they would not receive Him.
that it was the Lord's own will not to be received by the Samaritans,
for He was hastening to Jerusalem to suffer and to pour out His blood,
lest, held back by Samaritan welcome and the teaching of that people,
He should defer the day of His Passion, which He had come to endure.
consider: As the Samaritans resisted the Lord going up to Jerusalem,
so, if your mind reaches toward heaven, you will always have those who stir up quarrels, hatreds, and wars.
Yet let no vengeance creep into your mind on account of this;
but as far as you are able, strive to be of use.
If you see some standing in your way, do not rage in contentions;
but restrain yourself within yourself, and gather yourself together with your God, and say to Him:
Lord, I suffer violence, answer Thou for me.
Be not saddened by this, for they can do nothing except what God knows to be expedient at last for your good.
When tribulation comes, go out to meet so great a guest, receiving it kindly,
and say with a glad heart: Welcome, my friend, tribulation.
For whoever willingly suffers adversity for God is made like to Christ who suffered.
True salvation is in the cross, nor is any salvation found outside it.
Where there is no cross, that is, no tribulation, there is no patience;
and where there is no patience, neither can there be true salvation.
Know most certainly that all that befalls you, whether glad or sorrowful, is given you by God out of exceeding love.
But when the disciples, angered against the Samaritans, sought fire from heaven to consume them,
this vengeance, praised in Elias, was reproved by the Lord in them,
for they desired it not from love and the wish to correct, but from impatience and a craving for revenge.
Zeal avails nothing unless the will be discerning.
In the New Testament, which is of love, one must proceed gently and kindly,
not as in the Old, which is of fear, where men proceeded harshly and rigidly.
prayer: who didst not avenge Thyself on the Samaritans who denied Thee welcome,
but, bearing it patiently, didst humbly turn aside to another place:
grant to me, unworthy, O sweet guest of the soul,
that, all vices cast out, I may make ready a welcome pleasing to Thee in my heart;
and if ever, through my own deserts, Thou art not received there,
be not angry with me, nor turn away in wrath from Thy servant, nor take vengeance upon me;
but Thou, who camest not to destroy souls but to save them,
reconcile me to Thyself through Thy mercy,
and grant that I may open my heart to Thee by worthy preparation,
and that it may please Thee to enter in to me Thy servant, and mercifully to bless me. Amen.

latinJewel.latin: Cum igitur tribulatio advenerit, exi obviam hospiti tanto, benigne eum recipiendo; et dic corde jucundo: Bene venerit amica mea, tribulatio.
latinJewel.english: When therefore tribulation comes, go out to meet so great a guest, receiving it kindly; and say with a glad heart: Welcome, my friend, tribulation.
